title: Coupled Pendulums with Spring Energy Transfer
prompt:
Create a single-HTML-file Three.js application simulating two coupled pendulums connected by a spring, demonstrating energy transfer and beat frequency phenomena.

【Three.js Import (must follow exactly)】
Place in <head>:
<script type="importmap">
{ "imports": {
    "three": "https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/three@0.170.0/build/three.module.js",
    "three/addons/": "https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/three@0.170.0/examples/jsm/"
} }
</script>
Then in <script type="module">:
import * as THREE from 'three';
import { OrbitControls } from 'three/addons/controls/OrbitControls.js';

【Scene Setup】
- Background: dark gradient (#1a1a2e to #0f0f23)
- Camera: perspective, starting at (0, 5, 15) looking at (0, 0, 0)
- Lights: soft ambient (0.4 intensity), two point lights (warm and cool) for visual depth
- A horizontal support bar at y=8, extending from x=-5 to x=5 (gray cylinder)
- OrbitControls with damping

【Physics Model: Coupled Pendulums】
Two simple pendulums hang from the support bar:
- Pendulum A: pivot at (-2, 8, 0), length L_A (adjustable, default 5.0m), mass m_A = 1.0 kg
- Pendulum B: pivot at (+2, 8, 0), length L_B (adjustable, default 5.0m), mass m_B = 1.0 kg
- Gravity g = 9.81 m/s^2

A spring connects the two pendulums at a fraction (0.5) of their lengths from the pivot:
- Spring attachment points: at L_A*0.5 from pivot A, and L_B*0.5 from pivot B
- Spring rest length = distance between attachment points when both pendulums hang vertically
- Spring stiffness k (adjustable, default 5.0 N/m)
- Spring damping coefficient b = 0.01 (very small, to preserve energy transfer)

Equations of motion (small angle approximation NOT used -- use full nonlinear):
  For each pendulum i (i=A,B):
    I_i * alpha_i = -m_i * g * L_i * sin(theta_i) + tau_spring_i
  where I_i = m_i * L_i^2 (moment of inertia for point mass)
  tau_spring_i = torque from spring force applied at the attachment point

Spring force calculation:
  Let p_A = attachment point on pendulum A, p_B = attachment point on pendulum B
  displacement = |p_A - p_B| - restLength
  F_spring = -k * displacement (along the line p_A to p_B)
  Convert to torque on each pendulum using cross product with the pendulum arm

Numerical integration: RK4 with dt=0.002s, multiple substeps per frame.

Initial conditions: theta_A = 30 degrees (pi/6), theta_B = 0, omega_A = 0, omega_B = 0
This starts pendulum A swinging and energy gradually transfers to B through the spring.

【3D Visualization】
(1) Pendulum rods: cylinders, pendulum A in red (#FF4444), pendulum B in blue (#4444FF)
(2) Mass bobs: spheres (radius 0.3) at the end of each rod, matching rod colors
(3) Spring: rendered as a coiled line (sine wave along the connection) between attachment points. Use THREE.TubeGeometry or a custom BufferGeometry updated each frame. Spring color: green (#44FF44) when relaxed, yellow when stretched, orange when compressed.
(4) Support bar: gray cylinder at y=8
(5) Trajectory trails: fading lines showing recent positions of each bob (last 500 points)

【Energy Visualization】
Display energy bars on the right side of the screen (HTML overlay, not 3D):
- Pendulum A kinetic energy bar (red, id='bar-ke-a')
- Pendulum A potential energy bar (dark red, id='bar-pe-a')
- Pendulum B kinetic energy bar (blue, id='bar-ke-b')
- Pendulum B potential energy bar (dark blue, id='bar-pe-b')
- Spring potential energy bar (green, id='bar-spring')
- Total energy bar (white, id='bar-total')
Bars should be vertical, max height 200px, scaled to the total system energy.

【Beat Frequency】
When both pendulums have equal length and mass, energy transfers back and forth with a beat frequency:
  f_beat = (f_normal2 - f_normal1) / 2
The user should see pendulum A slow down as B speeds up, then reverse.

【Controls】
(1) Spring stiffness slider (id='slider-stiffness'): range 0.5 to 20, step 0.5, default 5. Higher stiffness = faster energy transfer.
(2) Pendulum A length slider (id='slider-length-a'): range 2 to 8, step 0.1, default 5.
(3) Pendulum B length slider (id='slider-length-b'): range 2 to 8, step 0.1, default 5.
(4) Keyboard: 'Space' to pause/resume, 'R' to reset to initial conditions, 'T' to toggle trail visibility.

All sliders in a bottom control panel with labels showing current values.

【HUD (id='hud')】
Semi-transparent dark panel at top-left:
- id='hud-theta-a': Pendulum A angle in degrees (1 decimal)
- id='hud-theta-b': Pendulum B angle in degrees (1 decimal)
- id='hud-energy-a': Total energy of pendulum A (2 decimals)
- id='hud-energy-b': Total energy of pendulum B (2 decimals)
- id='hud-total-energy': Total system energy (2 decimals)
- id='hud-sim-time': Simulation time in seconds (1 decimal)

【State Management】
Update window.__3D_STATE__ every frame:
{
  frameCount: number,
  thetaA: number,            // angle A in radians
  thetaB: number,            // angle B in radians
  omegaA: number,            // angular velocity A
  omegaB: number,            // angular velocity B
  energyA: number,           // KE + PE of pendulum A
  energyB: number,           // KE + PE of pendulum B
  springEnergy: number,      // PE stored in spring
  totalEnergy: number,       // total system energy
  springStiffness: number,   // current k value
  lengthA: number,           // current L_A
  lengthB: number,           // current L_B
  bobAPos: {x, y, z},       // world position of bob A
  bobBPos: {x, y, z},       // world position of bob B
  isPaused: boolean,
  simTime: number
}

【Energy Conservation】
Total energy E = KE_A + PE_A + KE_B + PE_B + Spring_PE should be conserved.
With b=0.01 damping, after 30 seconds, energy should decay less than 5%.
KE_i = 0.5 * m_i * (L_i * omega_i)^2
PE_i = -m_i * g * L_i * cos(theta_i) (taking pivot as zero reference)
Spring_PE = 0.5 * k * displacement^2

Output a single HTML file.
physics_constraints: Full nonlinear pendulum equations with spring coupling torque. RK4 integration dt=0.002s. Energy transfer via beat frequency. Spring force from displacement, converted to torque. Energy conservation within 5% over 30s with minimal damping. Initial: theta_A=pi/6, theta_B=0.
difficulty_analysis: L5: (1) Nonlinear coupled ODE system with spring torque coupling requires careful vector math; (2) Spring torque calculation from 2D/3D geometry; (3) RK4 over 4 coupled state variables; (4) Energy bar visualization synchronized with physics; (5) Beat frequency emerges only from correct coupling; (6) Spring visual as dynamic coil geometry; (7) Energy conservation depends on correct formulation of all energy terms.
